g.data-narration: Welcome to learning about pendant vertices, distances, and centres in tree data structures used in computer science and graph theory.
g.text: Pendant Vertex, Distance and Centres in a Tree

g.data-narration: A pendant vertex is a vertex with degree 1, meaning it connects to exactly one other vertex. Every tree with more than one vertex has at least two pendant vertices.
g.text: Definition: A vertex with degree 1 (connected to exactly one other vertex)
• Every tree with n > 1 vertices has at least 2 pendant vertices
• A tree with n vertices has exactly n-1 edges

g.data-narration: Distance between two vertices is the number of edges in the unique path connecting them. Since trees have no cycles, exactly one path exists between any pair.
g.text: Definition: The number of edges in the unique simple path connecting two vertices
• Distance from vertex to itself is 0

g.data-narration: Eccentricity of a vertex is the maximum distance to any other vertex. Radius is minimum eccentricity, diameter is maximum distance between any pair.
g.text: Eccentricity: Maximum distance from a vertex to any other vertex (ecc(v) = max{d(v,u)})
Radius: Minimum eccentricity among all vertices (r = min{ecc(v)})
Diameter: Maximum distance between any two vertices (d = max{d(u,v)})

g.data-narration: The centre of a tree consists of one or two vertices with minimum eccentricity. If two centres exist, they must be adjacent vertices.
g.text: Definition: Vertex or vertices with minimum eccentricity (equal to radius)
• A tree has either 1 centre or 2 centres
• If 2 centres, they are adjacent vertices
• Centre(s) minimize the maximum distance to any vertex

g.data-narration: Examples showing pendant vertices, eccentricities, centres via layer removal, diameter and radius in different tree structures.
g.text: Example 1: Tree A-B-C-B-D-E-F | Pendant: A, C, F | Ecc: A=4, B=3, D=2 | Centre = {D}
• If diameter = d, then radius = ceil(d/2)
